agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Memory leak from never-cleaned-up CourierTracker instances and WebSocket listeners is the confirmed cause of the steadily increasing memory usage."
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Memory leak: CourierTracker instances never released on disconnect",
      "risk": "Each courier reconnect (every ~5 minutes) creates a new CourierTracker with bound listeners, but no 'close' handler ever tears down the old instance. The WebSocket holds a reference to the CourierTracker via the listener closures, and if any external map/set holds a reference to the WebSocket or tracker, neither is ever GC'd. Over 3 months with frequent reconnects, this is a textbook unbounded memory leak.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — missing ws.on('close', ...) handler",
      "reproduction": "1. Connect a courier. 2. Disconnect after 5 min. 3. Reconnect (new CourierTracker created). 4. Repeat. Monitor process.memoryUsage().heapUsed — it grows monotonically and never reclaims old tracker allocations. At scale: (couriers × reconnects/day × 90 days) leaked objects.",
      "fix": "Add a 'close' handler that calls ws.removeAllListeners() and nulls out references. Ensure whatever creates CourierTracker instances also removes its own reference (e.g. delete from a Map<courierId, CourierTracker>). Consider a WeakRef or explicit dispose pattern.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service stability — this is the root cause of the reported memory growth"
    },
    {
      "title": "Unhandled JSON.parse throws on malformed WebSocket messages",
      "risk": "A single malformed message (binary frame, empty string, truncated JSON from flaky mobile network) crashes the entire process via an uncaught exception. In production with thousands of couriers, this is a availability-killing bug.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — JSON.parse(data) with no try/catch",
      "reproduction": "Send a non-JSON string (e.g., empty string or 'ping') over the WebSocket. JSON.parse throws, the 'message' listener has no try/catch, and the error propagates as an uncaught exception.",
      "fix": "Wrap in try/catch: try { const location = JSON.parse(data); } catch (e) { console.error('Malformed message', data); return; }. Also validate that lat/lng are finite numbers before passing to DB.",
      "time_to_fix": "10 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service availability — any malformed courier message can crash the process"
    },
    {
      "title": "Fire-and-forget DB write loses errors silently and has no backpressure",
      "risk": "db.couriers.updateLocation is called with no await and no .catch(). If the DB is slow or down, errors are silently swallowed as unhandled promise rejections (crash in Node 15+), and a fast-reconnecting courier can flood the DB with unbounded concurrent writes.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()",
      "reproduction": "1. Simulate DB latency (add 500ms delay). 2. Send location updates at 1/sec. 3. Observe: unhandled promise rejections pile up, and in Node 15+ the process terminates on unhandled rejection.",
      "fix": "Await or .catch() the DB call. Add rate-limiting/throttling so only the latest location is written per interval (e.g., at most once per second per courier). This also reduces DB load.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Data integrity and service stability under DB degradation"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No input validation on lat/lng coordinates",
      "description": "location.lat and location.lng are used directly from parsed JSON with no type or range checks. A courier sending {lat: null, lng: 'abc'} would write garbage to the DB. Validate both are finite numbers in valid geographic ranges (-90 to 90 lat, -180 to 180 lng).",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()"
    },
    {
      "title": "WebSocket 'error' handler only logs — does not close the socket",
      "description": "After a WebSocket error, the connection may be in an indeterminate state but is never explicitly closed, leaving a zombie connection that contributes to the memory leak.",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — ws.on('error', ...)"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Add a heartbeat/ping-pong mechanism to detect dead connections proactively rather than waiting for TCP timeout",
    "Throttle or debounce location writes to reduce DB pressure (couriers don't need sub-second persistence)",
    "Add structured logging (not console.error) with courier metadata for production observability"
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "The memory leak is actively degrading production. Fix should be deployed urgently but tested with a canary first to verify heap stabilizes.",
    "rollback_capability": "N/A — this is a bugfix, not a feature. Rollback would reintroduce the leak.",
    "backward_compatibility": "No breaking changes — fixes are internal to the class.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No monitoring exists. Add: process heap metrics, active WebSocket count, tracker instance count, DB write error rate.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": []
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert commit if fix introduces regressions, but the current code is already broken in production."
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Memory leak diagnosis — classic missing cleanup pattern with confirmed symptom (3 months of growth)",
      "JSON.parse crash — deterministic failure on any non-JSON input",
      "Unhandled promise rejection — confirmed behavior in modern Node.js"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "External lifecycle management — cannot see how CourierTracker instances are stored outside this class, but the leak exists regardless"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "db.couriers.updateLocation returns a Promise (standard for any DB client)",
      "A new CourierTracker is created per reconnect without disposing the old one (stated in prompt)"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this code for critical issues.

Key observations:
1. Memory leak: No cleanup of WebSocket listeners or references when connection closes. Each reconnect creates a new CourierTracker but old ones aren't cleaned up.
2. No JSON.parse error handling - will crash on malformed messages
3. No input validation on lat/lng
4. Fire-and-forget DB call with no error handling
5. No close listener on WebSocket

The memory leak is the critical finding given the context about steadily increasing memory usage over 3 months.
